speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today, March 22, 1995, testimony of Shari Braun.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shari.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shari Braun, interviewed by Carol Stulberg, Los Angeles, California United States of America. It will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Carol Stulberg. And I have the privilege of interviewing Shari Braun, March 22, 1995, in Los Angeles. Mrs. Braun, please tell us your name at birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] which means Shari, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miskolc.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hungary. What were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Malwin my mother name. And my father named Fulop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fulop was your father. And Marvin was your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Malwin, my mother, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Malwin Kalman, K-A-L-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have sisters and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister and one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Piroska, Piri, which means, Yiddish [INAUDIBLE]. And my brother is Rudy, Rudolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life as a child. What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came a very, very poor family, but very happy family. Unfortunately, my father died when I was two and a half years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what he did for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he was-- I, I don't know. Maybe salesperson. I don't know. I don't know. I never ask. I know what my mother did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was selling under, underwear, undershirt, underwear. And she traveled a lot. She went to the-- how you call that? Buggy, you know, it was no car those days. And she worked very hard alone. She raised three children alone. She never got married. And she tried to give us everything what she could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a happy life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- because we always get lots of love.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a religious school or a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish school. Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to an elementary school. It was nice. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with just Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Jewish children, maybe a couple children who were-- the parents were married in a different faith. And there was a elementary school. There was next to it, the middle school, like high school, and also, also, like a college for, for teachers who wanted to be teachers. And they were very nice. [INAUDIBLE] school, the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are a lot of Jewish families in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was all together 13,000 Jewish family. And 80,000 people lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a neighborhood with just Jewish? Or did you live with gentile people also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were gentile. It's very interesting, because we come-- I'm coming from a very religious family, orthodox family. And, and was just, I think, two other couple lived in the same block Jewish. They were all Christian. But we had a synagogue a few blocks away, which-- little temple, little synagogue, where my grandfather went every day, twice a day, maybe more. I don't know, but once for sure. He was an orthodox Jew. And maybe later on, you want to see the picture. I have it here. And I'm very proud of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year-- when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1930, January 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your religious life like at home, holidays and things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- excuse me, my voice. My mother tried to have Friday night, every Friday night, with a lit candle. He made cholet. He made chicken soup, and just Jewish kosher food. And it was not much. But was very tasty. And was for everybody a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have grandparents living nearby where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your mother or your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's side. He was in the same, same apartment-- how you call apartment? It's like-- it's-- there, there was not apartment house. It was like separate little houses, but it was in one complex. And he lived there. And he was very religious, and very wonderful person. He was an older man. What he done, he always studied the Talmud, read the Bible, prayed. And we learned a, a lot from him. He was very brave, very smart, very busy, very intelligent, and a good human being. And I can say the same thing about my mother. If he had-- if she had-- some-- if somebody came to the house and they was a beggar, let's say, didn't have something to eat, and we were just at the table, my mother get up. And he give-- and she give her food to the person. And she would not eat, because she said, somebody's more hungry, more needed. And we never had extra, because, because he did not-- she did not make as much-- excuse me, he and she, I-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. What was it like living among gentiles in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before when I was a small child, it was-- I had gentile girlfriends. And the neighbors were gentile. And they loved my mother. And I had no problem. We all had friend-- they were all friends of mine. And I had from the Jewish school Jewish friends also. I had no problem. But then they started the, the antisemitisms, then it's-- the friends started to be away from the Jewish people, because they saw the star, we-- we were. And, and they were just-- maybe were just taught by the parents. And the parents were not as close anymore to the-- to the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you remember the first signs of antisemitism and problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got-- when we put on the, the yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember exactly, probably beginning of '44. I think so. But I'm not sure. Maybe under '43. I have no-- not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before 1943, you lived relatively quietly in your city without any problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was going on in other parts of Europe with the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard that. But we heard two version. We heard that it was terrible what they're doing to the Jewish people. But in meantime, we heard that some people get card. And they said, they are fine. They took some place they have to work. But they have no problem. But they done to us the same thing in Auschwitz. When I was in Auschwitz, we had to fill out a card. And we had to write very nice to our relatives, if you had relatives, or the neighbors, how wonderful we are doing. And in meantime, lost already my mom, and my mother, and my grandfather. And, and, and was a nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you started having problems, and you started-- started to have to wear the star? What do you remember about that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the children were-- the friends were embarrassed to go with us. And they said, you know, my parents said, we better don't go with you because you're Jewish. And we cannot go with Jewish people. So it hurt, because we didn't do anything. Our sin was only we were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How else did your life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean over there in? It, it was hard, because you trusting people. You're trusting yours friends. And then you're-- they just turn your-- their, their back to you. It was hard. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother still able to work and support you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did work until last minute. Only the problem was they did not let Jewish people work, even what she done. She went to the market, sell her stuff. And this was not even allowed to Jewish people. Then men was not allowed-- and they knew him. Everybody knew him there. He was a very wonderful person, very popular person, good human being. And then he cannot-- he could not go-- excuse me, when she could not go, she asked me to go. I helped. I was the youngest. And-- but I had maybe bigger-- bigger mother. Or I don't know. I was always with my mother. I knew how she does things. She had to go to the city hall or, or somewhere to take care of things. She took me always with, with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did-- I did know quite a bit about business. And she give me the-- this time already, did not allow even Jewish people have new stuff to sell. So what she done, she bought old-- old clothes from people who are wealthy people, let's say.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and she give it to me. And I went out with a neighbor of mine, who was Christian lady. And, and I was selling for my mother. I skipped school. And we needed the money. We needed something to eat. And that's what-- yeah. And how I survive, I feel, because I had to work very hard at home. And nobody forced me. Nobody hit me. It, it was lovely, because I had my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have water. We had to go about at least two blocks to schlep the water, you know? And, and then we didn't have stove. We had to buy coal and wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: I chopped the wood. I chopped the coal. And I felt I was strong. If I would not do that, probably I would not be here today talking to you. Because those people in my age, first of all, most of them were killed. And even older people, I mean later teens or twenties, who were not strong, they were-- they were-- they could not take the labor. And I, to me, it was hard. But I still could pick up those cement, you know, and, and, and work in the field. And I was not afraid from the work, because I was used to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about the real trouble starting? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The real trouble, trouble started when they took us to the-- to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just came, the 'csendors' Do you know what is 'csendors' 'Csendors' is like police. But I think-- I don't know how to say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were the Hungarian gendarmes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gendarmes, yeah, we call 'csendors' Can I call 'csendors'?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, gendarmes. And they were not a-- not-- they were not good people. The police were not brutal. But they were very brutal. Even you won't do anything, but just you-- they know you are Jewish, they would do-- they will beat you up and do things which is unbelievable. And they came. And they said we had to get out of the house. So we went to a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto was in a Jewish neighborhood. And one of my cousin who was grown up and was already married, had an apartment there. And she took the family-- not just our family, but other family from-- it were our family. But my mother had about, I don't know how many, sisters, and, and cousins, and nephew, and nieces.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took-- she took as many people as she could. And we lived in this one bedroom house, and the floor, and wherever we can find a place. We were with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandfather went same area with a rabbi. And they had a lot of people. But those real religious people, they were together. And my grandfather-- we are not as far yet. But I want tell you what he done when I met him again before we went out to, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about that. I'll remind you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now when you get to the ghetto, you're living with a cousin in an apartment. And you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many families-- about how many people are in this one apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could not tell how many. How-- as many as could go in. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was four, six. At least 35 people, at least 35, 35 to 40 people. We were like herrings. We-- and one toilet what they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had no, no problem. And what we ate, what she had already saved it, you know. And she just shared with everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, a few weeks-- I don't know how many weeks or months. I tell the truth. I did not make notes. And I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- did you have to work at that time or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were volunteering to-- volunteered to go to work field-- to a field with my mother, my sister, and myself. And I think was about other 20 or 30 women there and girls. And we were working on the field. And I don't know, again, how many, maybe four weeks we were there, or how many weeks. Then they, they transferred us back, but not to the ghetto. They transferred us to the brick factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: I feel so embarrassed, because I'm so-- It's OK. Where was your brother at this time? Was he still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, no. They took him before they took us. He went with this-- I don't know how you call that. It's not to the concentration camp. They-- like a labor camp. And from the labor camp, we found out later he went to a concentration camp. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after being in this ghetto for awhile and living in this small apartment, tell me, was the ghetto walled in? Were you surrounded by walls while you lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: As, as remember, yes. As I remember, I only-- if not, I, I don't know about that. But I know that when we walked or woke up, we heard this-- how you call that? Shooting, but with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rifles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not rifle, this is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Machine guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Machine guns, thank you, machine guns over there. And probably nobody asked them. This was the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, unfortunately, Hungarians were not too good. I don't say everybody was bad. I'm sure you found a few nice people who hidden people. But, unfortunately, over there, more they killed, they were happier. They were big, very much against the, the, the Jewish people. They were very big anti, antisemite. So at the end of your few weeks in this ghetto, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to a, a-- we went to the labor camp. Then they-- before they took us the labor camp, they took us like in a stable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's sad to tell. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And one by one, girls and women was taken [SOBS] in. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SOBS] They examined us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Do you understand what I mean? I understand what you mean. Who examined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a general. And there was a woman. And the woman I know where is I come from. She was in a-- professional call girl. And, and she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a professional prostitute, do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, uh-huh. And she went, sorry, but she says she has to do it. And I was crying. And I didn't want to pull up my [SOBS] skirt. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And the general said to me, if you are not going to pull up your skirt, and you won't let pull up your panty, I'm going to go in with my-- she had this terrible, big stick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBS] with that stick, and she stepped close to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she had to examine us. And the whole examination was-- they thought-- they were telling because maybe the Jude-- maybe the Jewish people [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] were putting some jewelry in, you know? How could they do for a young girl who was a virgin? Not even go-- would not go out with a boy at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so rudely to us-- to us. Even the girl would-- would have been-- he was sym-- she was sympathetic. But she had to do it. And oh. I don't care how nicely you do it, or how careful you do it. You still a virgin. And going in with your hand up, you know, it was a terrible feeling. And I have to tell you. I'm not a youngster anymore. When I go to the gynecologist, she knows my story, this part of the story. And she's the most generous lady, because she knows what I went through. And, and she's very careful. Otherwise, I was always terrified to go to a gynecologist since this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was the Hungarians, the Hungarian police, and this professional--

speaker: interviewee
text: The 'csendor,' the 'csendor.'

speaker: interviewer
text: The gendarme and the professional prostitute who were examining the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever find anything in anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, nothing. This was, again, just to, to torture the people. And when I came out, the other people came to me. And they said, what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so white. And I was scared, and shaky. And, and my mother said, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And what my mother went through, I cannot tell you, because she was a wonderful mother. And she was always worried about her children-- not about her life, but her children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take your little sister in there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a big sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean your sister, your older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't remember exactly what happened to them. But I know what happened to me. But I know what happened with the others. So I know exactly what happened to them. But they didn't talk too much about it. And, and from there, they took us to, to the brick factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the brick factory? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought it's in Miskolc. But my brother-in-law said was not in Miskolc, close to Miskolc.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I cannot remember. And since I was in the concentration camp, I never went back to Hungary. I don't want to see them. I don't want to see the country, because it's not there. My mother is not there. [SOBS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the brick factory, what did they do with you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us in-- on the floor. And we were ready to-- they said just we are going to be there a couple days. And they are going to transfer us somewhere else. And men, and women, and boys, and girls, they were all together in the floor, like very close to-- you know. And, again, my mother was so worried about us. It's shameful or embarrassed, you know, my girls, men over there. And, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: But the most worry about her, what's going to happen with her daughters? This was the most worry. And we are always hear this machine gun, and, and things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the day we left, we were there, a man was walking around, looking around. And he said to us, don't you recognize me. I said, no. It was our grandfather. They took his-- he had to take his beard off, had to take the payots off. And he only-- I think he had a hat on. And he looked different. And he was so much optimism. She said-- he said, don't worry. Nothing will happen to you. And then he said, I want to pray for you. [SOBS] And he put-- [SOBS] few minute, please. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] He put his hand on my head. And he prayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bentshn, I don't know. You know bentshn? He benched me, and same with, with my mother-- with my sister. My mother was not there. I always said, if my mother would be there, probably she still be alive, but-- because I believe God, God listened to my grandfather prayer. So he prayed for us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Same, same day, you know. Every Friday night, he benched us. Where was your mother at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I can't-- I, I don't remember. We were just get up from there for this place was so small place. And maybe just we were going a little bit, looking around to maybe look for-- maybe look for, I'm sure for our grandfather. And he did the same thing. And he was in a different place, and was ready to take everybody to-- out to Auschwitz. We didn't know where we are going to go, who is going to take us, what's going to happen to us. But he found us. And he benched us. And I feel God helped to-- I think-- I think-- I know he was listening to his prayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this brick factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think just a few days. That's my feeling. I don't know exactly how long. If it's-- if three, four days, I have no idea. And then I had a, a table a thing to, to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how I'm going to do it? I wanted to be with my grandfather. I wanted to be with my mother. And the decision was very hard, because I loved them just the same. He was a wonderful human being. And she was a wonderful human being. And I said to myself, if I'm going to be with my grandfather, I won't be with my mother. And I was still a child, her child. But he's going to be alone. If I'm going to be with my mother, then he'll be alone. If I'm going with my grandmother-- grandfather, then my mother is going to worry about me. And I need her and my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it happened I had to go back to my mother. So-- and if I would not, I'm sure I wouldn't be here today, because as I understood, when we were-- when-- somebody told me when they were taking to Auschwitz, the plane went-- plane-- the train went to Auschwitz, the cattle car. Then my father died-- my grandfather died.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how. I don't know. He was sick. He was alive. But he was thrown out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the train. And somebody-- some, maybe 10 years ago, was here from Israel, same city where I came from. And she want to tell me. And when she went this far, I said, I don't want to hear it, what happened. I don't want to think back. I don't want to go. And I don't, because it's murder to look back what happened, innocent people, [SOBS] good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were taken out of the brick factory, you, and your mother, and your sister-- and you were separated from your grandfather-- how did you leave there?

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the brick factory, how did you leave? And where do you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They set us up. And we went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: With this cattle cart. And my mother was there. And my sister was there, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the cattle cars? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It seemed like ever. I don't know. Probably you know how long is it took a-- I don't know. I don't know. It was like for months. And I'm sure it was just few days, because it was very a lot of people. We could not even sit down. We could not turn around. Every-- we had a small window. We had, I think, 130 people. And people get sick because was no oxygen.
